source_intake_record.raw_question_text:
In the process of chip manufacturing, ion implantation is a crucial step. Ions are accelerated and enter a velocity selector horizontally, then pass through a magnetic analyzer, which selects ions with a specific charge-to-mass ratio. Subsequently, the ions are deflected by a deflection system and implanted into a wafer (silicon chip) lying in the horizontal plane. The uniform magnetic fields in the velocity selector, magnetic analyzer, and deflection system all have a magnetic induction magnitude of B, directed outward perpendicular to the plane of the paper. The uniform electric fields in the velocity selector and deflection system have an electric field magnitude of E, directed vertically upwards and outward perpendicular to the plane of the paper, respectively. The cross-section of the magnetic analyzer is a quarter-circular ring with inner and outer radii of $R_{1}$ and $R_{2}$, respectively, with small apertures at the center positions M and N at its ends. In the deflection system, the electric and magnetic fields are distributed within the same cube with side length L. The bottom of the deflection system is parallel to the horizontal plane of the wafer, and the distance between them is L as well. When no electric or magnetic field is applied to the deflection system, the ions are injected vertically onto point O on the wafer (which is the origin of coordinates in the figure, with the x-axis directed outward perpendicular to the paper). The entire system is in a vacuum, and the gravitational force on ions is negligible. The deflection angles of ions caused by the electric and magnetic fields are both small. When $\alpha$ is small, we have $\sin\alpha \approx \tan\alpha \approx \alpha$, and $\cos \alpha \approx 1-\frac{1}{2}\alpha^{2}$.

1. The magnitude of the velocity $v$ of the ions after passing through the velocity selector and the charge-to-mass ratio of the ions selected by the magnetic analyzer.
2. The position of the ion-implanted wafer, when only an electric field is applied in the deflection system, is represented by coordinates $(x, y)$.

3. The position where ions are implanted on the wafer when only a magnetic field is applied in the deflection system is represented by coordinates $(x, y)$.

4. Determine the position where the ions are implanted on the wafer, represented by coordinates $(x, y)$, when both electric and magnetic fields are applied simultaneously in the deflection system, and provide the justification.

image_dependency: Question refers to a figure-specific geometry: quarter-circular magnetic analyzer, apertures M and N, point O, and coordinate directions tied to the diagram
Image asset exists and registry_passed=true, so the visual reference is available for use
multi_step: The problem is explicitly split into four sub-questions with dependency across stages: velocity selector -> magnetic analyzer -> deflection system
Solving requires combining multiple physics relations across electric and magnetic fields, charge-to-mass selection, and trajectory/deflection reasoning
The final part asks for simultaneous electric and magnetic deflection with justification, indicating multi-condition reasoning rather than a single direct lookup
verifiability: answer_present=true with concrete symbolic outputs for all requested parts
Expected answers are closed-form expressions and coordinate pairs, which are in principle checkable by algebra or rule-based verification
Question states small-angle approximations explicitly, reducing ambiguity in the intended derivation framework

decision_reasons: Core assets are complete: question text, answer, and required image are all present, with registry_passed=true and no recorded asset issues.
Initial scoring is strong across image dependency, multi-step reasoning, and verifiability, indicating a high-value candidate.

Although the sample is diagram-orientation-sensitive and may have symbolic-equivalence checking risk, these are evaluation risks rather than missing-core-semantics risks, so they do not justify downgrading at collection time.

clean_problem_record.normalized_question_text:
In the process of chip manufacturing, ion implantation is a crucial step. Ions are accelerated and enter a velocity selector horizontally, then pass through a magnetic analyzer, which selects ions with a specific charge-to-mass ratio. Subsequently, the ions are deflected by a deflection system and implanted into a wafer (silicon chip) lying in the horizontal plane. The uniform magnetic fields in the velocity selector, magnetic analyzer, and deflection system all have a magnetic induction magnitude of B, directed outward perpendicular to the plane of the paper. The uniform electric fields in the velocity selector and deflection system have an electric field magnitude of E, directed vertically upwards and outward perpendicular to the plane of the paper, respectively. The cross-section of the magnetic analyzer is a quarter-circular ring with inner and outer radii of $R_{1}$ and $R_{2}$, respectively, with small apertures at the center positions M and N at its ends. In the deflection system, the electric and magnetic fields are distributed within the same cube with side length L. The bottom of the deflection system is parallel to the horizontal plane of the wafer, and the distance between them is L as well. When no electric or magnetic field is applied to the deflection system, the ions are injected vertically onto point O on the wafer, which is the origin of coordinates in the figure, with the x-axis directed outward perpendicular to the paper. The entire system is in a vacuum, and the gravitational force on ions is negligible. The deflection angles of ions caused by the electric and magnetic fields are both small. When $\alpha$ is small, we have $\sin\alpha \approx \tan\alpha \approx \alpha$, and $\cos \alpha \approx 1-\frac{1}{2}\alpha^{2}$.

1. Find the magnitude of the velocity $v$ of the ions after passing through the velocity selector and the charge-to-mass ratio of the ions selected by the magnetic analyzer.
2. Find the implantation position on the wafer, represented by coordinates $(x, y)$, when only an electric field is applied in the deflection system.
3. Find the implantation position on the wafer, represented by coordinates $(x, y)$, when only a magnetic field is applied in the deflection system.
4. Determine the implantation position on the wafer, represented by coordinates $(x, y)$, when both electric and magnetic fields are applied simultaneously in the deflection system, and provide the justification.

Find the magnitude of the velocity $v$ of the ions after passing through the velocity selector and the charge-to-mass ratio of the ions selected by the magnetic analyzer.
Find the implantation position on the wafer, represented by coordinates $(x, y)$, when only an electric field is applied in the deflection system.
Find the implantation position on the wafer, represented by coordinates $(x, y)$, when only a magnetic field is applied in the deflection system.

Determine the implantation position on the wafer, represented by coordinates $(x, y)$, when both electric and magnetic fields are applied simultaneously in the deflection system, and provide the justification.

answer_slots.target_text: Determine the implantation position on the wafer, represented by coordinates $(x, y)$, when both electric and magnetic fields are applied simultaneously in the deflection system, and provide the justification.

sentence_segments.text: In the process of chip manufacturing, ion implantation is a crucial step.
Ions are accelerated and enter a velocity selector horizontally, then pass through a magnetic analyzer, which selects ions with a specific charge-to-mass ratio.
Subsequently, the ions are deflected by a deflection system and implanted into a wafer (silicon chip) lying in the horizontal plane.
The uniform magnetic fields in the velocity selector, magnetic analyzer, and deflection system all have a magnetic induction magnitude of B, directed outward perpendicular to the plane of the paper.

The uniform electric fields in the velocity selector and deflection system have an electric field magnitude of E, directed vertically upwards and outward perpendicular to the plane of the paper, respectively.

The cross-section of the magnetic analyzer is a quarter-circular ring with inner and outer radii of $R_{1}$ and $R_{2}$, respectively, with small apertures at the center positions M and N at its ends.
In the deflection system, the electric and magnetic fields are distributed within the same cube with side length L.
The bottom of the deflection system is parallel to the horizontal plane of the wafer, and the distance between them is L as well.

When no electric or magnetic field is applied to the deflection system, the ions are injected vertically onto point O on the wafer, which is the origin of coordinates in the figure, with the x-axis directed outward perpendicular to the paper.
The entire system is in a vacuum, and the gravitational force on ions is negligible.
The deflection angles of ions caused by the electric and magnetic fields are both small.
When $\alpha$ is small, we have $\sin\alpha \approx \tan\alpha \approx \alpha$, and $\cos \alpha \approx 1-\frac{1}{2}\alpha^{2}$.
Find the magnitude of the velocity $v$ of the ions after passing through the velocity selector and the charge-to-mass ratio of the ions selected by the magnetic analyzer.
Find the implantation position on the wafer, represented by coordinates $(x, y)$, when only an electric field is applied in the deflection system.
Find the implantation position on the wafer, represented by coordinates $(x, y)$, when only a magnetic field is applied in the deflection system.

Determine the implantation position on the wafer, represented by coordinates $(x, y)$, when both electric and magnetic fields are applied simultaneously in the deflection system, and provide the justification.

node_records.canonical_value:
In the process of chip manufacturing, ion implantation is a crucial step. Ions are accelerated and enter a velocity selector horizontally, then pass through a magnetic analyzer, which selects ions with a specific charge-to-mass ratio. Subsequently, the ions are deflected by a deflection system and implanted into a wafer (silicon chip) lying in the horizontal plane. The uniform magnetic fields in the velocity selector, magnetic analyzer, and deflection system all have a magnetic induction magnitude of B, directed outward perpendicular to the plane of the paper. The uniform electric fields in the velocity selector and deflection system have an electric field magnitude of E, directed vertically upwards and outward perpendicular to the plane of the paper, respectively. The cross-section of the magnetic analyzer is a quarter-circular ring with inner and outer radii of $R_{1}$ and $R_{2}$, respectively, with small apertures at the center positions M and N at its ends. In the deflection system, the electric and magnetic fields are distributed within the same cube with side length L. The bottom of the deflection system is parallel to the horizontal plane of the wafer, and the distance between them is L as well. When no electric or magnetic field is applied to the deflection system, the ions are injected vertically onto point O on the wafer, which is the origin of coordinates in the figure, with the x-axis directed outward perpendicular to the paper. The entire system is in a vacuum, and the gravitational force on ions is negligible. The deflection angles of ions caused by the electric and magnetic fields are both small. When $\alpha$ is small, we have $\sin\alpha \approx \tan\alpha \approx \alpha$, and $\cos \alpha \approx 1-\frac{1}{2}\alpha^{2}$.

1. Find the magnitude of the velocity $v$ of the ions after passing through the velocity selector and the charge-to-mass ratio of the ions selected by the magnetic analyzer.
2. Find the implantation position on the wafer, represented by coordinates $(x, y)$, when only an electric field is applied in the deflection system.
3. Find the implantation position on the wafer, represented by coordinates $(x, y)$, when only a magnetic field is applied in the deflection system.
4. Determine the implantation position on the wafer, represented by coordinates $(x, y)$, when both electric and magnetic fields are applied simultaneously in the deflection system, and provide the justification.

In the process of chip manufacturing, ion implantation is a crucial step. Ions are accelerated and enter a velocity selector horizontally, then pass through a magnetic analyzer, which selects ions with a specific charge-to-mass ratio. Subsequently, the ions are deflected by a deflection system and implanted into a wafer (silicon chip) lying in the horizontal plane. The uniform magnetic fields in the velocity selector, magnetic analyzer, and deflection system all have a magnetic induction magnitude of B, directed outward perpendicular to the plane of the paper. The uniform electric fields in the velocity selector and deflection system have an electric field magnitude of E, directed vertically upwards and outward perpendicular to the plane of the paper, respectively. The cross-section of the magnetic analyzer is a quarter-circular ring with inner and outer radii of $R_{1}$ and $R_{2}$, respectively, with small apertures at the center positions M and N at its ends. In the deflection system, the electric and magnetic fields are distributed within the same cube with side length L. The bottom of the deflection system is parallel to the horizontal plane of the wafer, and the distance between them is L as well. When no electric or magnetic field is applied to the deflection system, the ions are injected vertically onto point O on the wafer, which is the origin of coordinates in the figure, with the x-axis directed outward perpendicular to the paper. The entire system is in a vacuum, and the gravitational force on ions is negligible. The deflection angles of ions caused by the electric and magnetic fields are both small. When $\alpha$ is small, we have $\sin\alpha \approx \tan\alpha \approx \alpha$, and $\cos \alpha \approx 1-\frac{1}{2}\alpha^{2}$.

1. Find the magnitude of the velocity $v$ of the ions after passing through the velocity selector and the charge-to-mass ratio of the ions selected by the magnetic analyzer.
2. Find the implantation position on the wafer, represented by coordinates $(x, y)$, when only an electric field is applied in the deflection system.
3. Find the implantation position on the wafer, represented by coordinates $(x, y)$, when only a magnetic field is applied in the deflection system.
4. Determine the implantation position on the wafer, represented by coordinates $(x, y)$, when both electric and magnetic fields are applied simultaneously in the deflection system, and provide the justification.

normalized_value.title: PhysReason 开放题
normalized_value.rewritten_question_text:
In the process of chip manufacturing, ion implantation is a crucial step. Ions are accelerated and enter a velocity selector horizontally, then pass through a magnetic analyzer, which selects ions with a specific charge-to-mass ratio. Subsequently, the ions are deflected by a deflection system and implanted into a wafer (silicon chip) lying in the horizontal plane. The uniform magnetic fields in the velocity selector, magnetic analyzer, and deflection system all have a magnetic induction magnitude of B, directed outward perpendicular to the plane of the paper. The uniform electric fields in the velocity selector and deflection system have an electric field magnitude of E, directed vertically upwards and outward perpendicular to the plane of the paper, respectively. The cross-section of the magnetic analyzer is a quarter-circular ring with inner and outer radii of $R_{1}$ and $R_{2}$, respectively, with small apertures at the center positions M and N at its ends. In the deflection system, the electric and magnetic fields are distributed within the same cube with side length L. The bottom of the deflection system is parallel to the horizontal plane of the wafer, and the distance between them is L as well. When no electric or magnetic field is applied to the deflection system, the ions are injected vertically onto point O on the wafer, which is the origin of coordinates in the figure, with the x-axis directed outward perpendicular to the paper. The entire system is in a vacuum, and the gravitational force on ions is negligible. The deflection angles of ions caused by the electric and magnetic fields are both small. When $\alpha$ is small, we have $\sin\alpha \approx \tan\alpha \approx \alpha$, and $\cos \alpha \approx 1-\frac{1}{2}\alpha^{2}$.

1. Find the magnitude of the velocity $v$ of the ions after passing through the velocity selector and the charge-to-mass ratio of the ions selected by the magnetic analyzer.
2. Find the implantation position on the wafer, represented by coordinates $(x, y)$, when only an electric field is applied in the deflection system.
3. Find the implantation position on the wafer, represented by coordinates $(x, y)$, when only a magnetic field is applied in the deflection system.
4. Determine the implantation position on the wafer, represented by coordinates $(x, y)$, when both electric and magnetic fields are applied simultaneously in the deflection system, and provide the justification.

agent_assessment.rationale: The problem statement is fully specified and the expected answers are present. The image is useful for the apparatus layout, field directions, radii, and wafer coordinates, and it is clear enough to support the text. Despite the problem being complex, the text-image pair is semantically understandable for annotation.

cleaning_records.decision_rationale: The sample is complete and jointly understandable, with usable rewrite output, clear answer targets, and high solvability. However, the alignment record is marked risky with a noted conflict, so it retains semantic value but should be checked before passing.

conflict_pairs.detail: 文本中的视觉锚点明显多于可识别视觉区域。

field_audit_records.before_value:
In the process of chip manufacturing, ion implantation is a crucial step. Ions are accelerated and enter a velocity selector horizontally, then pass through a magnetic analyzer, which selects ions with a specific charge-to-mass ratio. Subsequently, the ions are deflected by a deflection system and implanted into a wafer (silicon chip) lying in the horizontal plane. The uniform magnetic fields in the velocity selector, magnetic analyzer, and deflection system all have a magnetic induction magnitude of B, directed outward perpendicular to the plane of the paper. The uniform electric fields in the velocity selector and deflection system have an electric field magnitude of E, directed vertically upwards and outward perpendicular to the plane of the paper, respectively. The cross-section of the magnetic analyzer is a quarter-circular ring with inner and outer radii of $R_{1}$ and $R_{2}$, respectively, with small apertures at the center positions M and N at its ends. In the deflection system, the electric and magnetic fields are distributed within the same cube with side length L. The bottom of the deflection system is parallel to the horizontal plane of the wafer, and the distance between them is L as well. When no electric or magnetic field is applied to the deflection system, the ions are injected vertically onto point O on the wafer (which is the origin of coordinates in the figure, with the x-axis directed outward perpendicular to the paper). The entire system is in a vacuum, and the gravitational force on ions is negligible. The deflection angles of ions caused by the electric and magnetic fields are both small. When $\alpha$ is small, we have $\sin\alpha \approx \tan\alpha \approx \alpha$, and $\cos \alpha \approx 1-\frac{1}{2}\alpha^{2}$.

1. The magnitude of the velocity $v$ of the ions after passing through the velocity selector and the charge-to-mass ratio of the ions selected by the magnetic analyzer.
2. The position of the ion-implanted wafer, when only an electric field is applied in the deflection system, is represented by coordinates $(x, y)$.

3. The position where ions are implanted on the wafer when only a magnetic field is applied in the deflection system is represented by coordinates $(x, y)$.

4. Determine the position where the ions are implanted on the wafer, represented by coordinates $(x, y)$, when both electric and magnetic fields are applied simultaneously in the deflection system, and provide the justification.

Ions are accelerated and enter a velocity selector horizontally, then pass through a magnetic analyzer, which selects ions with a specific charge-to-mass ratio. Subsequently, the ions are deflected by a deflection system and implanted into a wafer (silicon chip) lying in the horizontal plane. The cross-section of the magnetic analyzer is a quarter-circular ring with inner and outer radii of $R_{1}$ and $R_{2}$, respectively, with small apertures at the center positions M and N at its ends. In the deflection system, the electric and magnetic fields are distributed within the same cube with side length L. The bottom of the deflection system is parallel to the horizontal plane of the wafer, and the distance between them is L as well. When no electric or magnetic field is applied to the deflection system, the ions are injected vertically onto point O on the wafer, which is the origin of coordinates in the figure, with the x-axis directed outward perpendicular to the paper. When $\alpha$ is small, we have $\sin\alpha \approx \tan\alpha \approx \alpha$, and $\cos \alpha \approx 1-\frac{1}{2}\alpha^{2}$.

2. Find the implantation position on the wafer, represented by coordinates $(x, y)$, when only an electric field is applied in the deflection system.

variants.rewritten_question_text:
In the process of chip manufacturing, ion implantation is a crucial step. Ions are accelerated and enter a velocity selector horizontally, then pass through a magnetic analyzer, which selects ions with a specific charge-to-mass ratio. Subsequently, the ions are deflected by a deflection system and implanted into a wafer (silicon chip) lying in the horizontal plane. The uniform magnetic fields in the velocity selector, magnetic analyzer, and deflection system all have a magnetic induction magnitude of B, directed outward perpendicular to the plane of the paper. The uniform electric fields in the velocity selector and deflection system have an electric field magnitude of E, directed vertically upwards and outward perpendicular to the plane of the paper, respectively. The cross-section of the magnetic analyzer is a quarter-circular ring with inner and outer radii of $R_{1}$ and $R_{2}$, respectively, with small apertures at the center positions M and N at its ends. In the deflection system, the electric and magnetic fields are distributed within the same cube with side length L. The bottom of the deflection system is parallel to the horizontal plane of the wafer, and the distance between them is L as well. When no electric or magnetic field is applied to the deflection system, the ions are injected vertically onto point O on the wafer, which is the origin of coordinates in the figure, with the x-axis directed outward perpendicular to the paper. The entire system is in a vacuum, and the gravitational force on ions is negligible. The deflection angles of ions caused by the electric and magnetic fields are both small. When $\alpha$ is small, we have $\sin\alpha \approx \tan\alpha \approx \alpha$, and $\cos \alpha \approx 1-\frac{1}{2}\alpha^{2}$.

1. Find the magnitude of the velocity $v$ of the ions after passing through the velocity selector and the charge-to-mass ratio of the ions selected by the magnetic analyzer.
2. Find the implantation position on the wafer, represented by coordinates $(x, y)$, when only an electric field is applied in the deflection system.

4. Determine the implantation position on the wafer, represented by coordinates $(x, y)$, when both electric and magnetic fields are applied simultaneously in the deflection system, and provide the justification.

open_ended_problem_variants.rewritten_question_text:
In the process of chip manufacturing, ion implantation is a crucial step. Ions are accelerated and enter a velocity selector horizontally, then pass through a magnetic analyzer, which selects ions with a specific charge-to-mass ratio. Subsequently, the ions are deflected by a deflection system and implanted into a wafer (silicon chip) lying in the horizontal plane. The uniform magnetic fields in the velocity selector, magnetic analyzer, and deflection system all have a magnetic induction magnitude of B, directed outward perpendicular to the plane of the paper. The uniform electric fields in the velocity selector and deflection system have an electric field magnitude of E, directed vertically upwards and outward perpendicular to the plane of the paper, respectively. The cross-section of the magnetic analyzer is a quarter-circular ring with inner and outer radii of $R_{1}$ and $R_{2}$, respectively, with small apertures at the center positions M and N at its ends. In the deflection system, the electric and magnetic fields are distributed within the same cube with side length L. The bottom of the deflection system is parallel to the horizontal plane of the wafer, and the distance between them is L as well. When no electric or magnetic field is applied to the deflection system, the ions are injected vertically onto point O on the wafer, which is the origin of coordinates in the figure, with the x-axis directed outward perpendicular to the paper. The entire system is in a vacuum, and the gravitational force on ions is negligible. The deflection angles of ions caused by the electric and magnetic fields are both small. When $\alpha$ is small, we have $\sin\alpha \approx \tan\alpha \approx \alpha$, and $\cos \alpha \approx 1-\frac{1}{2}\alpha^{2}$.

1. Find the magnitude of the velocity $v$ of the ions after passing through the velocity selector and the charge-to-mass ratio of the ions selected by the magnetic analyzer.
2. Find the implantation position on the wafer, represented by coordinates $(x, y)$, when only an electric field is applied in the deflection system.
3. Find the implantation position on the wafer, represented by coordinates $(x, y)$, when only a magnetic field is applied in the deflection system.
4. Determine the implantation position on the wafer, represented by coordinates $(x, y)$, when both electric and magnetic fields are applied simultaneously in the deflection system, and provide the justification.
